digest: The biggest story today is Anthropic's lawsuit against the Pentagon over being labeled a 'supply chain risk' - a move that could reshape AI-defense partnerships. Meanwhile, Claude Code users got a major productivity boost with the new /loop command for recurring tasks, while community discussions continue around Claude's controversial military applications.

stories.headline: Anthropic Sues Pentagon Over 'Supply Chain Risk' Classification
stories.summary: Anthropic has filed a lawsuit against the U.S. Department of Defense to block being blacklisted as a 'supply chain risk' [1][2][3]. The legal action challenges restrictions on AI use that could prevent government contracts and partnerships [4]. The Pentagon's classification appears related to concerns over AI applications in military operations.
key_points: Multiple news outlets report the lawsuit targets Pentagon blacklisting over AI use restrictions
The 'supply chain risk' label could significantly impact Anthropic's government business opportunities

sources.title: Anthropic sues US Government for calling it a risk
Anthropic Sues Pentagon over 'Supply Chain Risk' Label
Anthropic sues to block Pentagon blacklisting over AI use restrictions

stories.headline: Anthropic Partners with Mozilla on Firefox Security Initiative
stories.summary: Anthropic announced a new partnership with Mozilla focused on Firefox security [1]. The collaboration appears to leverage Claude's capabilities for browser security applications, though specific details of the initiative remain limited.

stories.headline: Claude Code Adds Recurring Task Automation with /loop Command
stories.summary: Claude Code v2.1.71 introduces the `/loop` command for running prompts or commands on recurring intervals, such as `/loop 5m check the deploy` [1]. The update also adds cron scheduling tools for session-based recurring prompts and a rebindable voice activation key [1]. Version 2.1.72 enhanced the feature with tool search activation and direct file writing capabilities [2].
key_points: New /loop command enables automated recurring tasks with flexible time intervals
Cron scheduling tools provide session-based automation capabilities
Voice activation key is now customizable through keybindings.json

stories.headline: Opus 4.6 Becomes Default with Medium Effort Setting
stories.summary: Claude Code now defaults Opus 4.6 to medium effort for Max and Team subscribers, positioning it as the optimal balance between speed and thoroughness [1]. Users can adjust this setting with the `/model` command, and the "ultrathink" keyword enables high effort for specific tasks [1]. Legacy Opus 4 and 4.1 models have been removed from the first-party API [1].
key_points: Medium effort is now the default setting, optimized for most common tasks
Legacy Opus models automatically migrate users to the newer 4.6 version

stories.headline: Claude Code Fixes API Compatibility Issues with Third-Party Gateways
stories.summary: Recent updates resolved API 400 errors when using `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` with third-party gateways by improving proxy endpoint detection [1]. The fixes also addressed compatibility issues with custom Bedrock inference profiles and non-standard model identifiers [1]. Additional improvements include better tool search functionality and reduced spurious error logging [2][3].

stories.headline: New Developer Tools Include Claude API Skill and Auto-Memory Features
stories.summary: Claude Code v2.1.69 added the `/claude-api` skill for building applications with the Claude API and Anthropic SDK [1]. Version 2.1.59 introduced automatic context saving to auto-memory and a `/copy` command with interactive code block selection [2]. Additional improvements include better bash command handling and enhanced remote control session naming [1][2].
key_points: New /claude-api skill streamlines API development workflows
Auto-memory feature automatically saves useful context for later reference
Interactive /copy command simplifies code block selection and management

stories.headline: Community Debates Anthropic's Claude Code Operating Costs
stories.summary: A detailed analysis challenges claims that Claude Code costs Anthropic $5,000 per user, sparking community discussion about the economics of AI tooling [1]. The post examines the actual infrastructure and operational costs behind the developer-focused service, generating significant engagement on Hacker News with 117 points and 62 comments [1].
key_points: Analysis disputes widely circulated $5k per user cost estimates
Community discussion focuses on realistic AI service economics

sources.title: No, it doesn't cost Anthropic $5k per Claude Code user

stories.headline: Military AI Applications Continue to Generate Controversy
stories.summary: Community discussions continue around Claude's involvement in military operations, with reports of AI assistance in targeting Iranian facilities generating significant debate [1][2]. Questions persist about the Pentagon's transparency regarding AI use in bombing operations, including incidents involving civilian targets [3]. The discussions reflect broader community concerns about AI ethics in warfare applications.
key_points: Reports suggest Claude AI helped coordinate strikes against 1,000 targets in 24 hours
Pentagon faces criticism for lack of transparency about AI involvement in military operations
Community debates focus on ethical implications of AI in warfare

sources.title: Palantir and Anthropic AI helped the US hit 1k Iran targets in 24 hours
Claude AI Helped Bomb Iran. But How Exactly?
Pentagon Refuses to Say If AI Was Used to Bomb Elementary School

stories.perspectives: The community remains divided on military AI applications - while some view them as legitimate defense tools, others express serious concerns about transparency and civilian safety in AI-assisted military operations.
